passages.explanation: Jesus is standing by the Lake of Gennesaret (the Sea of Galilee) with a crowd pressing around him to hear the word of God. He sees two boats at the water's edge — the fishermen are washing their nets. He gets into Simon's boat, asks him to push out a little, and teaches the crowd from the boat. When he finishes, he tells Simon: put out into deep water and let down the nets for a catch. Simon replies: master, we've worked hard all night and haven't caught anything. But because you say so, I will let down the nets. They catch such a large number of fish that the nets begin to break. They signal their partners in the other boat to help, and both boats are so full they begin to sink. Simon Peter falls at Jesus' knees: go away from me, Lord — I am a sinful man! Jesus says: don't be afraid. From now on you'll fish for people. They pull their boats up on shore, leave everything, and follow him. Peter's response isn't awe at the miracle — it's terror at who Jesus must be. The abundance of fish reveals the holiness of the person who commanded them.

In one of the towns, a man covered with leprosy sees Jesus, falls facedown, and begs: Lord, if you are willing, you can make me clean. Jesus reaches out and touches him — something no one would do to a leper, since contact made you ritually unclean. He says: I am willing. Be clean. Immediately the leprosy leaves. Jesus orders him to tell no one but to show himself to the priest and offer the sacrifices Moses commanded, as a testimony. But news about Jesus spreads all the more, and crowds gather to hear him and be healed. Jesus often withdraws to lonely places and prays. The touch is the point. Jesus doesn't need to touch him to heal him — he chooses to. The man hasn't been touched in years.

One day Jesus is teaching, and Pharisees and teachers of the law from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem are sitting there. The Lord's power is present to heal. Some men bring a paralysed man on a mat and try to get him in, but can't because of the crowd. They go up on the roof, remove tiles, and lower him down in front of Jesus. Seeing their faith, Jesus says: friend, your sins are forgiven. The Pharisees and teachers begin thinking: who is this who speaks blasphemy? Who can forgive sins but God alone? Jesus knows their thoughts and asks: which is easier — to say your sins are forgiven, or to say get up and walk? But to show you that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins — he tells the paralysed man — I tell you, get up, take your mat, and go home. The man gets up immediately, picks up what he was lying on, and goes home praising God. Everyone is amazed and filled with awe: we've seen remarkable things today. The physical healing proves the invisible reality — if Jesus can do what you can see, he can do what you can't see.

After this, Jesus goes out and sees a tax collector named Levi sitting at his tax booth. Follow me, Jesus says. Levi gets up, leaves everything, and follows him. Then Levi holds a great banquet for Jesus at his house. A large crowd of tax collectors and others are eating with them. The Pharisees and their teachers of the law complain to the disciples: why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners? Jesus answers: it's not the healthy who need a doctor, but the sick. I haven't come to call the righteous, but sinners to repentance. Tax collectors were despised as traitors — Jews collecting money for Rome, often skimming extra. Jesus doesn't clean Levi up first and then invite him. He calls him as he is.

They say to Jesus: John's disciples often fast and pray, and so do the disciples of the Pharisees, but yours go on eating and drinking. Jesus replies: can you make the friends of the bridegroom fast while he's with them? The time will come when the bridegroom will be taken from them, and in those days they will fast. He tells them a parable: no one tears a piece from a new garment to patch an old one — you'd ruin the new garment and the patch from the new won't match the old. No one pours new wine into old wineskins — the new wine would burst the skins, spilling the wine and ruining the skins. New wine must be poured into new wineskins. And no one after drinking old wine wants the new, for they say the old is good enough. Jesus isn't reforming the old system — he's bringing something entirely new. And he acknowledges the resistance: people who are comfortable with the old won't easily embrace the new.
